content.text: Determinism in LLM inference means that the same input always produces the same output. Achieving it requires eliminating every source of randomness in the pipeline: the decoding strategy, GPU floating-point non-determinism, and any dropout or stochastic regularisation at inference time. Determinism matters for reproducible evaluation, regression testing, and debugging — but it comes at the cost of output diversity and, on modern GPU hardware, sometimes at the cost of throughput.
temperature > 0, top-p, top-k draw from a random distribution
Set temperature=0 (greedy) or fix a random seed
Parallel reductions (softmax, matmul) produce different rounding errors across runs when thread scheduling varies
Batch padding changes which tokens appear in the attention mask; flash attention tiling differs
Use batch size=1 and consistent padding strategy
Some older models or custom architectures leave dropout active
Provider silently updates the model behind the same model ID
Pin a versioned model ID (e.g. claude-opus-4-8-20250514)

import random
import numpy as np
import torch
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM
import os


def set_deterministic_mode(seed: int = 42) -> None:
    """
    Set all random seeds and force deterministic GPU algorithms.
    Call this once before loading the model.

    Note: deterministic mode disables some CUDA optimisations and
    may reduce throughput by 10–40% on some operations.
    """
    random.seed(seed)
    np.random.seed(seed)
    torch.manual_seed(seed)
    torch.cuda.manual_seed_all(seed)

    # Force deterministic CUDA kernels where available.
    # Operations without a deterministic implementation will raise RuntimeError.
    torch.use_deterministic_algorithms(True)

    # Required for deterministic behaviour with cuBLAS (used in matmul)
    os.environ["CUBLAS_WORKSPACE_CONFIG"] = ":4096:8"

    # Disable cuDNN benchmarking (can select different algorithms across runs)
    torch.backends.cudnn.benchmark = False
    torch.backends.cudnn.deterministic = True


set_deterministic_mode(seed=42)

tok   = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("gpt2")
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("gpt2").eval()   # .eval() disables dropout

prompt = "The future of AI is"
input_ids = tok.encode(prompt, return_tensors="pt")

# Greedy (temperature=0) — deterministic regardless of seed
for _ in range(3):
    torch.manual_seed(42)    # seed the RNG even for greedy (no-op, but harmless)
    out = model.generate(input_ids, max_new_tokens=20, do_sample=False)
    print(tok.decode(out[0], skip_special_tokens=True))
# Identical output all three times

# Sampling with fixed seed — also reproducible
for _ in range(3):
    torch.manual_seed(42)    # reset before each call
    out = model.generate(
        input_ids,
        max_new_tokens=20,
        do_sample=True,
        temperature=0.8,
        top_p=0.9,
    )
    print(tok.decode(out[0], skip_special_tokens=True))
# Also identical — seed controls the PRNG used for sampling

parameter that seeds the server-side sampling RNG. Most providers document this as "best effort" — hardware differences and batching on the server side mean exact reproduction is not guaranteed across different calls or time periods, but within a short window the output is usually stable:

from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI()

prompt = "Write one sentence describing the colour blue."

# Same seed → usually same output (best-effort)
for i in range(3):
    resp = client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o-mini",
        seed=1234,              # server-side RNG seed (best effort)
        temperature=0.8,        # still sampling, but seeded
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    )
    print(f"Run {i+1}:", resp.choices[0].message.content)
    # system_fingerprint tells you which model/backend version was used:
    print("  system_fingerprint:", resp.system_fingerprint)
    # If system_fingerprint changes between calls, the model was updated.

# For true reproducibility, combine:
# - seed=<fixed integer>
# - temperature=0  (greedy)
# - a pinned model version
reproducible = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-4o-mini-2024-07-18",   # pinned version, not floating alias
    seed=42,
    temperature=0,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
)

import hashlib
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM
import torch


def generate_with_hash(
    model,
    tokenizer,
    prompt: str,
    seed: int = 42,
    max_new_tokens: int = 50,
) -> tuple[str, str]:
    """
    Generate text and return (output_text, sha256_hash).
    Store the hash in a test fixture to catch regressions.
    """
    torch.manual_seed(seed)
    input_ids = tokenizer.encode(prompt, return_tensors="pt")
    with torch.no_grad():
        output_ids = model.generate(
            input_ids,
            max_new_tokens=max_new_tokens,
            do_sample=False,   # greedy for maximum stability
        )
    text = tokenizer.decode(output_ids[0], skip_special_tokens=True)
    digest = hashlib.sha256(text.encode()).hexdigest()[:16]
    return text, digest


tok   = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("gpt2")
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("gpt2").eval()

text, digest = generate_with_hash(model, tok, "The transformer architecture")
print(f"Output: {text}")
print(f"Hash:   {digest}")

# In a test:
EXPECTED_HASH = "<stored from a known-good run>"
# assert digest == EXPECTED_HASH, f"Model output changed! Got {digest}"
use temperature=0 + fixed seed. Results must be reproducible to be meaningful.
hash greedy outputs against a golden fixture. A changed hash signals a model or dependency update.
change exactly one variable (prompt, model, temperature). Hold all seeds constant.
expect non-determinism at scale. Different server replicas will batch requests differently. If you need reproducibility per user, store the output, not the seed.
) in evaluation and testing. Floating aliases like

GPU floating-point non-determinism is subtle: on modern hardware, parallel reductions (matmul, softmax) produce results that differ by 1–2 ULPs (units in the last place) depending on thread scheduling. This is below human perception for any single output token, but can shift the argmax when two tokens have very similar probabilities — making greedy decoding non-reproducible without deterministic kernel settings. If reproducibility is critical, enforce it explicitly rather than assuming it.

attrs.alt: Determinism pipeline showing three columns: sources of non-determinism (sampling, GPU FP, API updates, batching), controls applied (greedy/seed, deterministic algorithms, version pinning, batch_size=1), and verification (SHA-256 hash tests, system_fingerprint checks)

content: Non-determinism in LLM outputs is invisible by default but costs teams significant time. Consider an evaluation benchmark: you run a model on 1,000 questions, get 78.3% accuracy, then run it again the next day and get 77.9%. Which number is correct? If the model was updated silently (floating alias), or if sampling was stochastic, both are correct snapshots of different systems — but you cannot tell. Reproducibility is the foundation of any scientific claim about model behaviour.
In production, determinism matters for different reasons:

content.text: (has a model update changed behaviour on a critical use case?),
(can you reproduce the exact sequence of tokens that produced a harmful output?), and
(you must change exactly one variable and hold everything else constant). Without determinism controls, every one of these workflows becomes unreliable.
The same model, prompt, and seed always produces the same score. Benchmark results are trustworthy and comparable across labs.
Hash the output of a key test case. If the hash changes, something changed — a new model version, a library update, a code change. The test catches it automatically.
When a user reports a bad output, you can replay the exact generation with the same seed and model version to investigate.
Changing exactly one variable (e.g., system prompt) while holding seed, model, and temperature constant gives a clean signal.

In regulated industries (finance, healthcare, legal), you may need to reproduce the exact output the model gave to a specific query weeks ago. Determinism — combined with logging — makes this possible.
Greedy decoding (temperature=0) always picks the single most likely token. For creative tasks (story generation, brainstorming), this produces repetitive, often inferior output compared to sampling.
torch.use_deterministic_algorithms(True) disables non-deterministic but faster CUDA kernels. Throughput can drop 10–40% for attention-heavy workloads.
A fixed seed on GPU A may produce different output on GPU B (different hardware, different floating-point arithmetic). Seeds are reproducible on the same machine and driver version only.
A deterministic output from PyTorch 2.0 may differ from PyTorch 2.1 if any operator's numerical implementation changed.
Hosted LLM APIs (OpenAI, Anthropic) cannot guarantee full determinism. Server-side batching, request routing, and hardware diversity introduce variation even with a fixed seed.

content: Greedy decoding is the primary mechanism for determinism. At each generation step, the model produces a logit vector of size |vocabulary| (typically 32k–128k values). Greedy decoding selects the token with the highest logit —

content.text: — which is a deterministic operation as long as the logits themselves are deterministic.

content: Sampling-based decoding introduces stochasticity: temperature scaling divides the logits by T before softmax, reshaping the distribution, then a random sample is drawn from it. With T → 0, the softmax distribution collapses to a one-hot on the argmax, recovering greedy behaviour:

GPU floating-point non-determinism arises from parallel reduction order. When computing softmax(logits), the GPU partitions the operation across thousands of CUDA threads. Different runs may schedule these threads differently, changing the order of floating-point additions. Since floating-point addition is not associative (a + (b + c) ≠ (a + b) + c in finite precision), the result can vary by 1–2 ULPs (units in the last place). When two logits are nearly equal, this 1 ULP difference can flip the argmax — making even greedy decoding non-reproducible without deterministic kernel settings.

content.text: When to Use Determinism (and When Not To)
Use full determinism (greedy + deterministic kernels + pinned model) for:
Automated evaluation benchmarks where you report numerical scores
Regression test suites that run on every code push (CI/CD)
Debugging and root cause analysis of specific failures
Compliance and audit logging in regulated industries
Research experiments where reproducibility is a scientific requirement
Stories, poems, brainstorming. Diversity is a feature — use higher temperature and varied seeds.
Different servers may give slightly different outputs. This is fine — store the actual output, not the seed.
Run the same question multiple times with sampling and take a majority vote. Stochasticity is exploited to reduce variance.
Generate diverse paraphrases or synthetic examples for training. Non-determinism increases diversity of the generated corpus.
Example 1: Verifying greedy determinism across multiple runs

import torch
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM
import hashlib

def text_hash(text: str, length: int = 12) -> str:
    return hashlib.sha256(text.encode()).hexdigest()[:length]

tok   = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("gpt2")
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("gpt2").eval()

prompt = "The quick brown fox"
input_ids = tok.encode(prompt, return_tensors="pt")

outputs = []
for run in range(5):
    with torch.no_grad():
        out_ids = model.generate(
            input_ids,
            max_new_tokens=30,
            do_sample=False,      # greedy
        )
    text = tok.decode(out_ids[0], skip_special_tokens=True)
    h = text_hash(text)
    outputs.append(h)
    print(f"Run {run+1}: hash={h}")

assert len(set(outputs)) == 1, "Not deterministic!"
print("All 5 runs produced identical output ✓")
Example 2: Reproducible sampling with a fixed seed

import torch
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM

tok   = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("gpt2")
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("gpt2").eval()

prompt = "Once upon a time"
input_ids = tok.encode(prompt, return_tensors="pt")

SEED = 1337
results = []

for run in range(3):
    torch.manual_seed(SEED)     # reset PRNG before EACH call
    with torch.no_grad():
        out_ids = model.generate(
            input_ids,
            max_new_tokens=30,
            do_sample=True,
            temperature=0.8,    # sampling enabled
            top_p=0.95,
        )
    text = tok.decode(out_ids[0], skip_special_tokens=True)
    results.append(text)
    print(f"Run {run+1}: {text[:80]}")

assert results[0] == results[1] == results[2], "Seed not controlling output!"
print("Fixed seed → identical sampled outputs across 3 runs ✓")

In production ML teams, evaluation benchmarks run nightly on every model checkpoint. Here is a complete harness that enforces full determinism and raises an alert when a model's output changes unexpectedly:

"""
deterministic_eval.py — Production evaluation harness with hash-based regression detection.
Run: python deterministic_eval.py --mode baseline   (first run: store hashes)
     python deterministic_eval.py --mode check      (subsequent runs: compare)
"""
import json, hashlib, os, argparse, random
import numpy as np
import torch
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM


GOLDEN_FILE = "golden_hashes.json"
TEST_CASES = [
    "What is the capital of France?",
    "Explain the difference between machine learning and deep learning.",
    "Write a one-sentence definition of entropy.",
    "What year was the Transformer architecture introduced?",
    "Summarise the attention mechanism in two sentences.",
]


def set_seeds(seed: int = 42) -> None:
    random.seed(seed)
    np.random.seed(seed)
    torch.manual_seed(seed)
    torch.cuda.manual_seed_all(seed)
    # Enforce deterministic CUDA kernels
    torch.use_deterministic_algorithms(True, warn_only=True)
    os.environ["CUBLAS_WORKSPACE_CONFIG"] = ":4096:8"
    torch.backends.cudnn.benchmark = False


def generate(model, tok, prompt: str, seed: int = 42, max_new_tokens: int = 80) -> str:
    torch.manual_seed(seed)
    ids = tok.encode(prompt, return_tensors="pt")
    with torch.no_grad():
        out = model.generate(ids, max_new_tokens=max_new_tokens, do_sample=False)
    return tok.decode(out[0], skip_special_tokens=True)


def sha8(text: str) -> str:
    return hashlib.sha256(text.encode()).hexdigest()[:8]


def run_eval(model, tok):
    set_seeds()
    results = {}
    for case in TEST_CASES:
        output = generate(model, tok, case)
        results[case] = sha8(output)
        print(f"  [{results[case]}] {case[:50]}...")
    return results


def main():
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument("--mode", choices=["baseline", "check"], default="check")
    args = parser.parse_args()

    tok   = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("gpt2")
    model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("gpt2").eval()

    print("Running evaluation...")
    current = run_eval(model, tok)

    if args.mode == "baseline":
        with open(GOLDEN_FILE, "w") as f:
            json.dump(current, f, indent=2)
        print(f"\nBaseline hashes saved to {GOLDEN_FILE}")
        return

    # Check mode: compare against golden
    with open(GOLDEN_FILE) as f:
        golden = json.load(f)

    failures = []
    for case in TEST_CASES:
        if current.get(case) != golden.get(case):
            failures.append(f"  CHANGED: {case[:50]}")
            failures.append(f"    golden={golden.get(case)}  current={current.get(case)}")

    if failures:
        print("\nREGRESSION DETECTED:")
        for line in failures:
            print(line)
        exit(1)
    else:
        print("\nAll hashes match golden. No regression. ✓")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
Not resetting the seed before each generation call:
If you call torch.manual_seed(42) once at the start and then generate 5 times, each generation advances the PRNG state differently. Reset the seed immediately before each call you want reproducible.

Without torch.use_deterministic_algorithms(True), GPU floating-point reductions can produce different argmax results when two logit values are extremely close. Temperature=0 is necessary but not sufficient for full reproducibility.
Using floating model aliases in evaluations:
gpt-4o, claude-3-5-sonnet-latest, and similar aliases update silently. Always use versioned model IDs for evaluations you intend to reproduce later.
In production, seeds are not portable — they may produce different outputs on different hardware or software versions. Store the actual generated text in your database.
Expecting seeds to work across different backends:
A PyTorch seed is specific to PyTorch's PRNG. If you switch from PyTorch to ONNX Runtime or TensorFlow, the same seed will not reproduce the same output.

import torch
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM

tok   = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("gpt2")
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("gpt2").eval()
ids   = tok.encode("Hello world", return_tensors="pt")

# WRONG: seed set once, but PRNG state advances with each call
torch.manual_seed(42)
out1 = model.generate(ids, max_new_tokens=20, do_sample=True, temperature=0.8)
out2 = model.generate(ids, max_new_tokens=20, do_sample=True, temperature=0.8)
# out1 != out2 because the seed was consumed by out1's sampling

# CORRECT: reset seed before each call you want reproducible
torch.manual_seed(42)
out_a = model.generate(ids, max_new_tokens=20, do_sample=True, temperature=0.8)
torch.manual_seed(42)
out_b = model.generate(ids, max_new_tokens=20, do_sample=True, temperature=0.8)
assert (out_a == out_b).all(), "Should be identical"
print("Correctly reproducible ✓")
Separate evaluation concerns from production concerns:
Use strict determinism for offline evaluation; accept non-determinism in production serving. Never use evaluation infrastructure to make real-time decisions.
Record both model version and system_fingerprint:
For API calls, log model ID, system_fingerprint, and temperature alongside the output. The fingerprint changes when the backend model or infrastructure is updated.
Use hash-based golden files in CI:
A failing hash is a signal — investigate before updating the golden. The change may be intentional (model improvement) or a bug (numerical regression).
In your eval README: list the seed, torch version, CUDA version, model ID, and deterministic flag settings. Future you will thank present you.
For API providers, use the lowest available temperature (0.0) plus a seed:
Even if the provider's seed support is best-effort, combining temperature=0 with a seed gives the best reproducibility possible given the provider's infrastructure constraints.

content: Greedy decoding is the fastest decoding strategy — one argmax per step, no sampling overhead. However, enabling

content.text: can reduce throughput significantly because it disables CUDA kernels that use non-deterministic atomic operations. For a production evaluation harness running on 10,000 questions, this is acceptable. For a real-time API serving 1,000 requests/second, it may not be.

content: At scale, the practical approach is: run evaluations on a dedicated GPU with deterministic mode enabled, and serve production requests on a separate fleet without the overhead. The two environments need not agree byte-for-byte — they just need to use the same model version.

content.text: The canonical way to enforce reproducible CUDA operations. Set warn_only=True first to identify which operations lack deterministic implementations.
Supports do_sample=False (greedy), and honours torch.manual_seed() for sampling. The generate() docstring documents all decoding parameters.
Best-effort server-side seed. Returns system_fingerprint in response headers — check this to detect silent model updates.
Does not currently expose a seed parameter. Use temperature=0 for maximum stability, and pin versioned model IDs.
Open-source framework for LLM benchmarking. Implements deterministic generation by default and logs all hyperparameters for reproducibility.
Scope the model and tokenizer to the session (autouse=True, scope='session') to avoid reloading between tests. Set seeds in a session-scoped autouse fixture.

# conftest.py — pytest setup for deterministic model tests
import pytest
import os
import torch
import random
import numpy as np
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM

@pytest.fixture(scope="session", autouse=True)
def set_deterministic():
    """Force deterministic mode for the entire test session."""
    random.seed(42)
    np.random.seed(42)
    torch.manual_seed(42)
    torch.use_deterministic_algorithms(True, warn_only=True)
    os.environ["CUBLAS_WORKSPACE_CONFIG"] = ":4096:8"
    yield

@pytest.fixture(scope="session")
def model_and_tokenizer():
    tok   = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("gpt2")
    model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("gpt2").eval()
    return model, tok


# test_generation.py
def test_greedy_is_deterministic(model_and_tokenizer):
    model, tok = model_and_tokenizer
    ids = tok.encode("Hello", return_tensors="pt")

    torch.manual_seed(42)
    out1 = model.generate(ids, max_new_tokens=10, do_sample=False)
    torch.manual_seed(42)
    out2 = model.generate(ids, max_new_tokens=10, do_sample=False)

    assert (out1 == out2).all(), "Greedy decoding must be deterministic"

content: Beam search is an alternative deterministic strategy: it maintains k candidate sequences at each step and selects the k most probable continuations. Beam search is deterministic for the same reasons as greedy (no sampling), but it has O(k × beam_width) memory overhead. For evaluation purposes where you need a single deterministic output, greedy (beam_size=1) is simpler and faster. Use beam search when you need higher quality output and can afford the compute (e.g., machine translation).

content.text: A deterministic pipeline can reproduce incorrect or harmful outputs exactly. Determinism is a debugging tool, not a safety mechanism. Combine it with content filtering and output validation.
If users can observe output tokens and infer the seed (e.g., by querying the model many times), they can predict future outputs. In adversarial settings, randomise seeds per request.

If you log model outputs for compliance, store the full output text (not just a hash or seed). Hashes can collide; seeds are not portable. Store: timestamp, model_id, model_version, input, output, parameters.
When you intentionally update a model, the golden hashes will change. Review and approve these changes explicitly — do not auto-update golden files in CI without human review.

Deterministic results on GPU X may differ on GPU Y (different architecture, different cuBLAS version). If your evaluation must run on multiple hardware targets, specify the exact hardware in the reproducibility documentation.
temperature=0, torch.manual_seed(), torch.use_deterministic_algorithms(True), and CUBLAS_WORKSPACE_CONFIG must all be set.
Greedy decoding (temperature=0) is the primary tool
for deterministic generation — it always picks the argmax token, requiring no random sampling.
Even greedy decoding can vary by 1 ULP without deterministic kernel settings, flipping the argmax when two logits are near-equal.
Reset the seed before each call,
not just once at startup — the PRNG state advances with each generation.
No hosted API guarantees exact reproducibility. Use temperature=0 + seed + versioned model ID for maximum stability.
Floating aliases (gpt-4o, claude-3-5-sonnet-latest) update silently. Always use a dated version for any evaluation you need to reproduce.
Store outputs, not seeds, in production:
Seeds are not portable across machines or software versions. The actual generated text is what matters.
Hash-based golden tests in CI catch regressions automatically
— a changed hash signals a model or dependency update and requires human review.
